one_line_takeaway: REPO detoxifies LLMs by erasing toxic information from token-level hidden representations via a domain-adversarial preference objective (rather than only shifting output likelihoods like DPO/NPO), yielding lower toxicity and markedly better robustness to relearning, orthogonalization, and enhanced-GCG attacks than DPO, NPO, RMU, and Circuit Breakers, at no measurable cost to perplexity, F1, or MMLU accuracy on GPT-2 Small/Medium and Gemma-2B.
key_finding: On GPT-2 Small in-distribution (PairToxicity), REPO achieves the lowest forget-set toxicity (0.0961) vs. NPO (0.1392), DPO (0.1506), and RMU (0.1527), while retain-set toxicity and perplexity stay close to reference; forget-sample perplexity rises sharply (18.1 to 70.8), consistent with erasing toxic information rather than just suppressing it. OOD, REPO gets the lowest RealToxicityPrompts score (0.21) vs. next-best NPO (0.24) while matching reference WikiText perplexity (23.6). Under adversarial attacks (Table 1, GPT-2 Medium and Gemma-2B): for GPT-2 Medium under relearning on PairToxicity, REPO's post-attack toxicity is 0.119 (forget) vs. 0.148 for both NPO and DPO; under enhanced-GCG (RealToxicity), REPO reaches 0.208 vs. 0.389 (RMU) and 0.347 (NPO); under orthogonalization REPO again scores lowest. The same ordering holds on Gemma-2B (e.g., enhanced-GCG RealToxicity: REPO 0.217 vs. DPO 0.269, NPO 0.472, CB 0.428, RMU 0.358). MMLU accuracy is essentially unchanged across all methods (REF 0.418, REPO 0.422, DPO 0.422, NPO 0.423, CB 0.418, RMU 0.411), indicating detoxification does not degrade general capability. Ablations (Table 3, GPT-2 Small) show REPO's representation-space + token-level design is responsible for the gains: CE-only retain loss gives minor toxicity reduction (0.2824 to 0.2367) but hurts perplexity (28.0 to 45.8); a SURE-style adversarial objective improves toxicity further (0.2021) but still underperforms REPO (0.1020) and has worse perplexity (34.0 vs. 28.2, matching reference's 28.0). Table 4 shows a two-layer discriminator beats a one-layer/linear discriminator on toxicity reduction (0.1020 vs. 0.1356 on forget set) at negligible extra compute cost. Mechanistic analysis (Figs. 4-7) shows REPO's edits are concentrated in deeper layers and localized to toxic tokens and toxicity-aligned neurons, whereas DPO/NPO edits are diffuse across tokens/layers and CB/RMU edits, while also deep, are not token-localized -- offered as the explanation for REPO's robustness advantage.
core_contribution: The paper introduces REPO (Representation Erasure-based Preference Optimization), a method for detoxifying LLMs that reformulates detoxification as a token-level, pairwise preference problem operating in representation space rather than output/likelihood space. For each prompt, a preferred non-toxic continuation (retain, x_r) and dispreferred toxic continuation (forget, x_f) are used to train the model with two combined losses: (1) a token-wise KL 'retain anchoring' loss keeping the edited model close to the frozen reference model on the retain sequence, and (2) a token-level domain-adversarial 'representation erasure' loss (a small 2-layer MLP discriminator attached via a gradient-reversal layer to the final transformer block's hidden states) that trains the LLM's features to make retain and forget token representations indistinguishable to the discriminator. Unlike DPO/NPO, which shift output-space likelihoods, REPO directly erases the internal features that let the LM head distinguish toxic from benign continuations at the token level, which the authors argue produces deeper, more localized, and more durable edits.
threat_model: The paper treats detoxification as an unlearning/robust-alignment problem and evaluates against three adversarial recovery strategies drawn from prior unlearning-robustness literature (Hu et al. 2024; Lucki et al. 2025): (1) Relearning attacks -- fine-tuning the already-detoxified model on either 10 forget-set examples or 1000 retain-set examples to see if toxic behavior resurfaces; (2) Orthogonalization attacks -- computing a per-layer 'unlearned direction' (mean activation difference between reference and unlearned model on the forget set) and projecting it out of hidden states at inference time to restore toxicity; (3) Enhanced GCG -- an adversarial-suffix optimization attack (building on Zou et al. 2023 GCG) modified to use the frozen reference model as a 'malicious teacher,' aligning the unlearned model's hidden representations at selected layers with the reference model's via a distillation loss, specifically designed to defeat representation-based unlearning defenses. The adversary in all three cases has either fine-tuning access (relearning) or white-box access to model weights/activations (orthogonalization, enhanced GCG); the paper does not consider black-box prompting attacks as its primary robustness axis.
limitations: The paper's own scope limits: (1) All quantitative results use small/medium-scale models (GPT-2 Small/Medium, Gemma-2B); no results are shown on larger, more capable, or RLHF/instruction-tuned models -- the abstract fragment even notes 'We did not include RLHF-tuned models because REPO is robust against those attacks compared to DPO and NPO,' i.e., that comparison is asserted rather than empirically shown in the extracted evaluation tables. (2) Robustness is evaluated against three specific attack families (relearning, orthogonalization, enhanced-GCG); no black-box prompt-only jailbreak sweep or many-attempt human red-teaming is reported. (3) Toxicity is measured solely via the Perspective API, a single automated classifier, without human validation of REPO's own outputs. (4) The domain-adversarial/gradient-reversal training approach (built on DANN and the authors' own prior SURE method) is computationally similar to standard fine-tuning-based unlearning but requires paired retain/forget continuations for the same prompt, which may not be trivial to construct for other harm categories beyond toxicity. (5) The 'Discussion' and 'Impact Statement' explicitly flag that the value of the approach depends on 'whose preferences are being optimized' -- an acknowledged, unaddressed limitation of preference-based detoxification generally.

quantitative_results: GPT2-Small in-distribution forget toxicity: REPO 0.0961 vs NPO 0.1392, DPO 0.1506, RMU 0.1527
GPT2-Small OOD RealToxicityPrompts score: REPO 0.21 vs NPO 0.24 (next-best), WikiText PPL REPO 23.6 (matches reference)
GPT2-Medium relearning attack, PairToxicity forget: REPO 0.119 vs NPO/DPO 0.148
GPT2-Medium enhanced-GCG, RealToxicity: REPO 0.208 vs RMU 0.389, NPO 0.347
Gemma-2B enhanced-GCG, RealToxicity: REPO 0.217 vs DPO 0.269, CB 0.428, RMU 0.358, NPO 0.472
MMLU accuracy on Gemma-2B: REF 0.418, REPO 0.422, DPO 0.422, NPO 0.423, CB 0.418, RMU 0.411
Table 3 ablation (GPT2-Small): REF toxicity 0.5123/PPL 28.04; CE-only 0.4454/PPL 45.77; SURE 0.3750/PPL 34.04; REPO 0.1913/PPL 28.23
Table 4 discriminator ablation: one-layer disc. forget toxicity 0.1356 vs two-layer disc. 0.1020, PPL ~28.2-28.3 for both
